html:
Ebba Magnusdotter Brahe, born on March 16, 1596, at Lerjeholm, and died on January 5, 1674, in Stockholm, was a Swedish industrialist, entrepreneur, lady-in-waiting, and countess. On June 24, 1618, she married Field Marshal Jakob De la Gardie, with whom she had children including her son Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie and daughter Maria Sofia De la Gardie.

Ebba Brahe is known for having been in love with King Gustav II Adolf before his marriage, and they both desired to marry each other, a dream that never came true. This romance has been the subject of many depictions within the romantic-historical tradition in Sweden, and it is documented in their correspondence.

== Early Years == Ebba Brahe was born as the daughter of Count Magnus Brahe and Brita Stensdotter Leijonhufvud. She served as a lady-in-waiting to Dowager Queen Kristina of Holstein-Gottorp from 1611 to 1614, and to Dowager Queen Katarina Stenbock from 1614 to 1618. She came to the court after her mother's death to complete her education. She was described as a beauty and attracted the attention of, among others, Duke Johan of Östergötland.

== Connection with Gustav II Adolf == At the court, she became acquainted with King Gustav II Adolf. The two fell in love and wanted to get married. The first letter preserved from their relationship is dated March 6, 1613; they had clearly been in love for some time. In this letter, the king stated that he was waiting for her to inform her father that he had proposed to her. The Queen Dowager, the King's mother Kristina, opposed the marriage plans. She did not believe that Ebba Brahe had a high enough social status and preferred that her son enter into a politically advantageous marriage with a member of a ruling dynasty. She also treated Brahe poorly as her lady-in-waiting, harassing her and trying to convince her that she could not trust Gustav Adolf's words. This caused Brahe's father to refuse his consent. The King courted her and sent her gifts through Lars Sparre, a chamberlain, who acted as an intermediary. He also tried to persuade his mother with the help of his half-sister Katarina, Duke Henry Julius of Saxony, and Chancellor Dr. Nils Chesnecopherus. Kristina finally gave her consent on the condition that the couple agreed to a period of reflection of a few years. They continued their correspondence during the war. Kristina continued to try to persuade Brahe, who on October 10, 1614, wrote: "It does not befit a young woman of Her Majesty's royal standing to aspire to such things." That same year, she was transferred from Kristina's service to Katarina Stenbock, likely to move her away from the castle in Stockholm. In the spring of 1615, it became clear that Kristina would never relent, and that Brahe also seemed to have been persuaded by her, which forced Gustav II Adolf to abandon his plans. The King's relationship with Margareta Slots is believed to have finally convinced Brahe, who at this time supposedly wrote her famous quote on a windowpane in Stockholm Castle: "I am content with my lot and thank God for His grace."

That same year, she received a proposal of marriage from Field Marshal Jakob De la Gardie, and two years later, she accepted: On November 11, 1617, their engagement was celebrated at Katarina Stenbock's house in Stockholm. They married on June 24, 1618, in Stockholm, in the presence of the two Queen Dowagers and the King's brother.

== Marriage == The relationship between Ebba Brahe and De la Gardie is described as happy, and she herself was considered a "kind wife and a good mother." Her husband served as governor of Estonia and Livland from 1619 to 1628, and during that time, Ebba Brahe lived with him there. She gave birth to fourteen children, seven of whom died in infancy. The most successful of her children was her son, Magnus Gabriel.

During her marriage, Ebba Brahe became involved in various activities, where she demonstrated her competence, primarily focusing on managing her family's financial interests. Because her husband was often preoccupied with his career and frequently absent on military campaigns, she took responsibility for the family's estates and developed her business acumen by traveling between the family's properties in both Sweden and the Baltic region. She is described as a capable and wise landowner and businesswoman.

In 1628, she and her husband permanently moved back to Sweden. The couple built, among other things, "Makalös" in Stockholm and owned Läckö, Ekholmens Castle, Arnö, Sundholmens Castle, Jakobsdals Castle, Runsa Castle, and Axholmen in the parish of Fläckebo. As a member of the nobility, she was known for her elegance and taste for luxury in clothing, art, food, and jewelry. She was a customer at Claude Roquette's luxury department store in Stockholm and maintained a court-like household with approximately one hundred people. She was famous for her interest in flowers and animals, having gardens with experimental flower and fruit cultivation, and keeping animals such as pigeons, parrots, and turtles. Among her friends was Margareta Boije, who was married to the Lord High Chancellor Arvid Forbus. In his final years, her husband suffered greatly from an illness that caused him to go blind, and caring for him increasingly occupied her time.

== Industrialist == In 1652, Ebba Brahe became a widow. After her husband's death, the residential palace was inherited.

How certain are we about this?

People from the era of Swedish Great Power were extensively documented in archives and contemporary publications. Most information about who they were and what they did is well-documented; the uncertainty lies in interpretation and motivation.
tldr: Ebba Brahe was a wealthy noblewoman who became known for her deep love for King Gustav II Adolf, whom she could never marry. She later married the Marshal of the Realm and became a successful businesswoman, managing the family's extensive estates.
lattlast:
Ebba Brahe was a woman of noble birth who lived in the 17th century. She worked at the royal court, meaning she was close to the royal family and the queens. During this time, she fell in love with King Gustav II Adolf. They wrote letters to each other and wanted to marry, but the king's mother prevented their plans from happening because she felt that Ebba did not have a high enough social status.

After it became impossible to marry the king, Ebba met a man named Jakob De la Gardie. He was a high-ranking official, known as a "riksmarsk." They married in 1618 and had many children together. Because her husband was often away working, Ebba took responsibility for managing the family's large estates and farms.

Ebba became very skilled at running businesses and managing finances, which made her a successful businesswoman. She lived luxuriously with the help of numerous servants and was known for her taste in art and jewelry. She was also interested in gardens and animals such as parrots and turtles.
